steps.step:
Create remote cluster by running:
export CLUSTER_NAME="qe-olnester"
export SSH_PUB_KEY="$(ssh-keygen -y -f ~/.ssh/libra.pem)"
export PULL_SECRET="$(cat ${HOME}/config.json)"

oc process -f config/templates/cluster-deployment.yaml \
   CLUSTER_NAME="${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
   SSH_KEY="${SSH_PUB_KEY}" \
   PULL_SECRET="${PULL_SECRET}" \
   BASE_DOMAIN=qe.devcluster.openshift.com \
   AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="${aws_access_key_id}" \
   AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="${aws_secret_access_key}" \
   | oc apply -f -

Create template for configmap configmap.yaml:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: foo
  namespace: default
data:
  foo: bar
Go to the remote cluster and run "oc create configmap.yaml" and check results "oc get configmap -n default"
steps.expectedResult:
[onest@localhost go_openshift]$ oc get configmap -n default
NAME      DATA      AGE
foo      1         55s

steps.step:
Go back to origin cluster and create syncset-patch.yaml template with :
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
kind: SyncSet
metadata:
  name: test-patch
spec:
  clusterDeploymentRefs:
  - name: testoleg
  patches:
  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    name: foo
    namespace: default
    patch: |-
      { "data": { "foo": "new-bar" } }
    patchType: merge

and save changes
Run "oc create -f syncset-patch.yaml"
steps.expectedResult:
[onest@localhost go_openshift]$ oc create -f syncset-patch.yaml
syncset.hive.openshift.io/test-patch created

steps.step: Go to the remote cluster and check results: "oc get configmap foo -o yaml"
steps.expectedResult:
[onest@localhost go_openshift]$ oc get configmap -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: v1
  data:
    foo: new-bar
  kind: ConfigMap
  metadata:
    creationTimestamp: 2019-04-11T14:58:09Z
    name: foo
    namespace: default
    resourceVersion: "106779"
    selfLink: /api/v1/namespaces/default/configmaps/foo3
    uid: 38a3b936-5c6a-11e9-87fc-0e9682aef3ce
kind: List
metadata:
  resourceVersion: ""
  selfLink: ""

steps.step:
Create cluster deployment
export CLUSTER_NAME="qe-olnester"
export SSH_PUB_KEY="$(ssh-keygen -y -f ~/.ssh/libra.pem)"
export PULL_SECRET="$(cat ${HOME}/config.json)"

oc process -f config/templates/cluster-deployment.yaml \
   CLUSTER_NAME="${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
   SSH_KEY="${SSH_PUB_KEY}" \
   PULL_SECRET="${PULL_SECRET}" \
   BASE_DOMAIN=qe.devcluster.openshift.com \
   AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="${aws_access_key_id}" \
   AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="${aws_secret_access_key}" \
   | oc apply -f -

Create syncset.yaml file template with the next data:
[cloud-user@preserve-olnester-workstation 320]$ cat syncset.yaml
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1alpha1
kind: SyncSet
metadata:
  name: test-syncset
spec:
  clusterDeploymentRefs:
  - name: oleg
  resourceApplyMode: "Sync"
  resources:
  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    metadata:
      name: foo1
      namespace: default
    data:
      foo: bar1

  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    metadata:
      name: foo2
      namespace: default
    data:
      foo: bar2
Run "oc create -f syncset.yaml" and check creation "oc get syncset"
steps.expectedResult:
[onest@localhost go_openshift]$ oc get syncset
NAME           AGE
test-syncset   2m5s

steps.step: Login yo the remote cluster "qe-olnester" and go to the project "default"
Run "oc get configmap"
steps.expectedResult:
oc [onest@localhost ~]$ oc get configmap
NAME      DATA      AGE
foo1      1         18s
foo2      1         17s

steps.step:
Edit syncset resource in origin cluster
"oc edit syncset test-syncset"

Delete next data:
  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    metadata:
      name: foo2
      namespace: default
    data:
      foo: bar2
and save changes
Go to the remote cluster and check results: run "oc get configmap"
steps.expectedResult:
Confgimap "foo2" is deleted

[onest@localhost ~]$ oc get configmap
NAME      DATA      AGE
foo1      1         11m

steps.step:
2. Create and apply a SelectorSyncSet configuration file.

% cat SelectorSyncSet-2040.yaml
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
kind: SelectorSyncSet
metadata:
  name: httperror-configmaps
spec:
  clusterDeploymentSelector:
    matchLabels:
      httperror: enabled
  resourceApplyMode: Sync
  resources:
  - apiVersion: v1
    kind: ConfigMap
    metadata:
      name: custom-error-pages-test
      namespace: openshift-config
    data:
      404.html: |
        <html><body><h1>My Custom 404 Page</h1></body></html>
      503.html: |
        <html><body><h1>My Custom 503 Page</h1></body></html>

% oc apply -f SelectorSyncSet-2040.yaml

Wait for the SyncSet to be applied. Then log in to the spoke cluster and run the following command to verify the field has been configured:
steps.expectedResult:
% oc get selectorsyncset httperror-configmaps --kubeconfig=hub.kubeconfig
NAME                   AGE
httperror-configmaps   145m


% oc get cm -n openshift-config custom-error-pages-test --kubeconfig=spoke.kubeconfig
NAME                      DATA   AGE
custom-error-pages-test   2      39s

  • Configure ClusterDeployment.spec.platform.gcp.discardLocalSsdOnHibernate

  • Apply Manifests

steps.step:
B: Clusterpool
Deploy hive with image IMG=quay.io/2uasimojo/hive:gcp-ssd make deploy
1.To prepare a clusterpool manifests file and configure the worker machine type, follow these steps:
  • Generate ClusterPool Manifests
  • Generate install-config yaml file
  • Modify the install-config.compute.platform.gcp.type
  • Create install config secret
  • Configure ClusterPool.spec.platform.gcp.discardLocalSsdOnHibernate
  • Configure ClusterPool.spec.installConfigSecretTemplateRef
  • Save the File
  • Apply Manifests

$ cat gcp-ic/install-config.yaml
additionalTrustBundlePolicy: Proxyonly
apiVersion: v1
baseDomain: qe.gcp.devcluster.openshift.com
compute:
- architecture: amd64
  hyperthreading: Enabled
  name: worker
  platform:
    gcp:
      type: a2-ultragpu-1g
      onHostMaintenance: Terminate
  replicas: 1
$ oc create secret generic my-install-config-template --from-file=install-config.yaml=./gcp-ic/install-config.yaml
secret/my-install-config-template created

$ cat mihuang2693-pool.yaml
apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  kind: ClusterPool
  metadata:
    creationTimestamp: null
    name: mihuang2693
  spec:
    installConfigSecretTemplateRef:
      name: my-install-config-template
    skipMachinePools: true
    baseDomain: qe.gcp.devcluster.openshift.com
    hibernationConfig: null
    imageSetRef:
      name: mihuang2693-imageset
    platform:
      gcp:
        discardLocalSsdOnHibernate: false
        credentialsSecretRef:
          name: mihuang2693-gcp-creds
        privateServiceConnect:
          enabled: false
        region: us-central1
    pullSecretRef:
      name: mihuang2693-pull-secret
    size: 1

steps.step:
Here is an example of a SyncSet for applying a ConfigMap to all clusters.

$ cat syncset.yaml
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
kind: SyncSet
metadata:
  name: mihuang-syncset
spec:
  clusterDeploymentRefs:
  - name: mihuangpsc-0729
  resourceApplyMode: "Upsert"
  enableResourceTemplates: true
  resources:
  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    metadata:
      name: cm1
      namespace: default
    data:
      platform: the platform is {{ fromCDLabel "hive.openshift.io/cluster-platform" }}
      region: the region is {{ fromCDLabel "hive.openshift.io/cluster-region" }}
      using-hiveutil: if using hiveutil {{fromCDLabel "hive.openshift.io/hiveutil-created"}}
      version: version is {{fromCDLabel "hive.openshift.io/version"}}
      cluster-name1: Cluster name is {{ fromCDLabel "cluster-name" }}
  - apiVersion: v1
    kind: ConfigMap
    metadata:
      name: cm2
      namespace: default
    data:
      cluster-name1: Cluster name is {{ fromCDLabel "cluster-name" }}
      version: version is {{fromCDLabel "hive.openshift.io/version"}}

steps.step:
4. Remove maintenanceMode and scale the MachinePool controller via：
(Note: .spec.maintenanceMode and .spec.controllersConfig cannot coexist within the HiveConfig.)

spec:
  controllersConfig:

  • worker node: 3

steps.step: Generate your own serving certificate and configure Hive to use it. Use the following instruction https://docs.google.com/document/d/19S1dA7ATXUos7dKZGtpnBiGidXhx--JX1lLgPgVj4Jo

Download and install certbot for letsencrypt
For instructions, go to https://certbot.eff.org/ , pick ‘Others’ for the first dropdown and your OS for the second dropdown.

Create a directory on your local machine to hold the letsencrypt certificates and required subdirectories:
$ mkdir -p ~/letsencrypt/{config,logs,work}

Create a new certificate for your cluster. For a 4.x cluster, you will need a certificate for the api endpoint and for the router wildcard.
$ cd ~/letsencrypt
$ certbot certonly --config-dir ./config --work-dir ./work --logs-dir ./logs \
  --preferred-challenges dns --manual

steps.step:
This shows how to add DNS record in the last step, you need to click the create record button

use these two commands to check if the DNS record was created successfully.

dig _acme-challenge.apps.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com TXT
dig _acme-challenge.api.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com TXT

You can now create a secret for your serving certificate with oc:
oc create secret tls foo-serving-cert \
--cert=$HOME/letsencrypt/config/live/api.foo.new-installer.openshift.com/fullchain.pem \
--key=$HOME/letsencrypt/config/live/api.foo.new-installer.openshift.com/privkey.pem
steps.expectedResult:
[suyue@fedora letsencrypt]$ oc create secret tls foo-serving-cert --cert=/home/suyue/letsencrypt/config/live/api.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com/fullchain.pem --key=/home/suyue/letsencrypt/config/live/api.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com/privkey.pem
secret/foo-serving-cert created

steps.step:
One of the files that certbot creates when generating a new certificate is a chain.pem file (located in the same directory as the cert and private key files in step above. The chain.pem files contains a CA cert that can be used to validate certificates generated by letsencrypt. In order to configure it, first create a secret with it in the hive namespace:
$ oc create secret generic letsencrypt-ca -n hive --from-file=ca.crt=~/letsencrypt/config/live/api.foo.new-installer.openshift.com/chain.pem
After creating the secret, edit hiveconfig with:
$ oc edit hiveconfig hive
And add a reference to the secret in the config spec:
spec:
  additionalCertificateAuthorities:
  - name: letsencrypt-ca
steps.expectedResult:
[suyue@fedora letsencrypt]$ oc create secret generic letsencrypt-ca -n hive --from-file=ca.crt=$HOME/letsencrypt/config/live/api.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com/chain.pem
secret/letsencrypt-ca created
[suyue@fedora letsencrypt]$ oc edit hiveconfig hive
hiveconfig.hive.openshift.io/hive edited

steps.step:
1. Generate a new clusterdeployment using hiveutil and save the yaml to a local file:
    $ ./bin/hiveutil create-cluster foo --base-domain new-installer-openshift.com -o yaml > /tmp/cluster.yaml
  2. Edit the clusterdeployment in /tmp/cluster.yaml by adding a certificate bundle entry under the clusterdeployment’s spec ( the name is arbitrary and is only used for reference within the clusterdeployment … in this case we’re using foocert, the secret ref must point to the secret you created in step 6 above:
      spec:
        certificateBundles:
        - name: foocert
          secretRef:
            name: foo-serving-cert
  3. Configure the control plane serving certs by editing controlPlaneConfig under spec and point to the certificate bundle defined in step 2:
        controlPlaneConfig:
          servingCertificates:
            default: foocert
  4. Configure the default ingress by adding an entry for the default ingress to the cluster deployment spec:
      spec:
        ingress:
        - name: default
          domain: apps.foo.new-installer.openshift.com
          servingCertificate: foocert
  5. Create the cluster with `oc apply`:
    $ oc apply -f /tmp/cluster.yaml

  spec:

    certificateBundles:

    clusterName: sguo-cert
    controlPlaneConfig:
      servingCertificates:
        default: foocert
    ingress:
    - name: default
      domain: apps.sguo-cert.qe.devcluster.openshift.com
      servingCertificate: foocert
.....

clusterimageset.hive.openshift.io/sguo-cert-imageset configured

steps.step:
Once the cluster finishes installing, it will apply the necessary configuration changes to the control plane and ingress so that the new certificates are in place. This may take a few minutes after installation is complete.
You can validate that the new cluster’s control plane is configured with the letsencrypt certificates by curling its endpoint:

$ curl -v https://api.foo.new-installer.openshift.com:6443
You should not get any complaint from curl about not being able to validate the certificate and in the verbose output, you should see that the certificate has been signed by LetsEncrypt
For the router certificate, you can simply go to the cluster’s console (URL should be in ClusterDeployment’s status). If the browser doesn’t complain about self-signed certificates, then you are using the right certificate.

steps.step:
Hive should now be generating a kubeconfig that includes LetsEncrypt’s CA so that it can connect to the target server without certificate errors.
Extract the kubeconfig:
$ oc get secret foo-admin-kubeconfig -o jsonpath=’{ .data.kubeconfig }’  | base64 -d > /tmp/cluster.kubeconfig


And use it to communicate with the cluster:
$ export KUBECONFIG=/tmp/cluster.kubeconfig
$ oc get clusteroperators

Create cluster deployment with overriden CLUSTER_IMAGE_SET parameter

export CLUSTER_NAME="onesttest"
export SSH_PUB_KEY="$(ssh-keygen -y -f ~/.ssh/libra.pem)"
export PULL_SECRET="$(cat ${HOME}/config.json)"

oc process -f config/templates/cluster-deployment.yaml \
   CLUSTER_NAME="${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
   SSH_KEY="${SSH_PUB_KEY}" \
   PULL_SECRET="${PULL_SECRET}" \
   BASE_DOMAIN=qe.devcluster.openshift.com \
   AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="${aws_access_key_id}" \
   AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="${aws_secret_access_key}" \
   CLUSTER_IMAGE_SET=openshift-v4.0.0-0.8 \
   | oc apply -f -

steps.step: Scenario 2: Set annotation before create cluster ,add annotation to yaml template.

Create a cluster .

bin/hiveutil create-cluster mihuang17551 --base-domain qe.devcluster.openshift.com --release-image quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.10.20-x86_64 --ssh-private-key-file ~/.ssh/id_rsa --ssh-public-key-file ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub --region us-east-2 -o yaml > dep17551.yaml

Add annotation to yaml template,and wait cluster install successful.

apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  kind: ClusterDeployment
  metadata:
    annotations:
      hive.openshift.io/powerstate-pause: "true"

$oc create -f dep17551.yaml
steps.expectedResult:
###Observe the status of the cd, PowerState becomes unknown  as soon as CD is provisioned.and the PowerState becomes unknown.

steps.step: Scenario 3:Set annotation for a claimed cluster

Create a pool and claim a cluster
$ bin/hiveutil clusterpool create-pool mihuangpool-1755 --base-domain qe.devcluster.openshift.com --release-image quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.10.20-x86_64 --size 2 --region us-east-2 -o yaml > deppool1755.yaml
$ oc create -f deppool1755.yaml
$ bin/hiveutil clusterpool claim mihuangpool-1755 mihuang-claim

Add annotation to the running cluster from clusterpool.
$ oc patch cd mihuangpool-1755-tvpvf -n mihuangpool-1755-tvpvf--type 'merge' -p '{"metadata": {"annotations": {"hive.openshift.io/powerstate-pause": "true"}}}'

steps.step:
1.$ git reset --hard 1566d49af21982b916eaafa166460f3f5ca29764
   $ IMG=quay.io/lwan0/hive:1566d49 make deploy

2.Create a vsphere cluster. (refer to  )

./bin/hiveutil create-cluster mihuangvsvs1 --base-domain=qe.devcluster.openshift.com --cloud=vsphere --release-image registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.11.0-0.nightly-2022-05-10-045003 --vsphere-api-vip 172.31.248.97 --vsphere-ca-certs /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/02265526.0:/etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/2835d715.0:/etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/6b99d060.0  --vsphere-datacenter ''SDDC-Datacenter'' --vsphere-cluster 'Cluster-1' --vsphere-default-datastore 'WorkloadDatastore'  --vsphere-ingress-vip 172.31.248.99 --vsphere-network 'qe-segment' --vsphere-vcenter 'vcenter.sddc-44-236-21-251.vmwarevmc.com' -o yaml > y.yaml

$oc create -f y.yaml

steps.step:
4.create a machinepool

cat machinepoolvs1.yaml
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
kind: MachinePool
metadata:
  name: mihuangvsvs1-infra
  namespace: default
spec:
  clusterDeploymentRef:
    name: mihuangvsvs1
  name: infra
  platform:
    vsphere:
      coresPerSocket: 1
      cpus: 2
      memoryMB: 8192
      osDisk:
        diskSizeGB: 120
  replicas: 1


$ oc create -f machinepoolvs1.yaml
$ oc get machinepool

Check the Controller's log about mihuangvs1-infra ,will show the error about decoder.

steps.step:
create a new syncset.yaml file and add there next code:
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
kind: SyncSet
metadata:
  name: test-syncset
spec:
  clusterDeploymentRefs:
  - name: oleg
  resources:
  - kind: ConfigMap
    apiVersion: v1
    metadata:
      name: foo
      namespace: default
    data:
      foo: bar

Where:
  - name: test-syncset - resource name
  - name: oleg - the name of the just created remote cluster
  - kind: ConfigMap - type of the resource
  - name: foo - name of this new resource
  - namespace: default - the namespace name of the remote cluster where this resouce will be created

steps.step: Navigate to GCP's web console, make sure each infra VM of the spoke cluster has all Shielded VM features (Secure Boot, vTPM and Integrity Monitoring) enabled.
Also check the infra MachineSets of the spoke cluster. Make sure secureBoot: Enabled.

fxie-mac:hive fxie$ oc get machineset -l hive.openshift.io/machine-pool=infra --kubeconfig fxie-hive-2.kubeconfig -A -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.template.spec.providerSpec.value.shieldedInstanceConfig}' | jq
{
  "secureBoot": "Enabled"
}

  "secureBoot": "Enabled"
}

steps.step:
Scenario 1:
With the annotation hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image="true", installerImageOverride set

1.Prepare a ClusterDeployment provision configuration file, like:
$./bin/hiveutil create-cluster jshucluster \
--base-domain=qe.azure.devcluster.openshift.com \
--cloud=azure \
--azure-base-domain-resource-group-name=os4-common \
--release-image=quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.10.30-x86_64 -o yaml > 2014.yaml

2.Get installer pullspec you want to override with it, run:
jianpingshu@jshu-mac HIVE-2014 % oc adm release info quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.11.3-x86_64 --pullspecs | grep installer
Warning: the default reading order of registry auth file will be changed from "${HOME}/.docker/config.json" to podman registry config locations in the future version of oc. "${HOME}/.docker/config.json" is deprecated, but can still be used for storing credentials as a fallback. See https://github.com/containers/image/blob/main/docs/containers-auth.json.5.md for the order of podman registry config locations.
 installer                   quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:139926a3c3e93b89fa82c84b668029ee9619c8805e898267062ea5878b6a3c7d

3. Edit above created configuration file,set annoation

$ cat 2014.yaml
apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  kind: ClusterDeployment
  metadata:
    annotations:
      hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image: "true"
 ......
     provisioning:
    imageSetRef:
      name: jshucluster-imageset
    installConfigSecretRef:
      name: jshucluster-install-config
    installerImageOverride: jshu.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:139926a3c3e93b89fa82c84b668029ee9619c8805e898267062ea5878b6a3c7d
  pullSecretRef:
    name: jshucluster-pull-secret
4.install cluster ,check if cd.Status.CLIImage domain copy from cd.Status.InstallerImage domain.
steps.expectedResult:
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster -o json | jq -r ".status.installerImage"
jshu.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:139926a3c3e93b89fa82c84b668029ee9619c8805e898267062ea5878b6a3c7d
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster -o json | jq -r ".status.cliImage"
jshu.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:9f92a648938f9a6a07c8af2f6a2532d94dacfc5076d609c30526a71deeee2a59

steps.step:
Scenario 2:
With the annotation hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image="false", installerImageOverride set

1.Create cluster ,did not add annotation,and set the installerImageOverride.

$ ./bin/hiveutil create-cluster --cloud=aws jshucluster1 --base-domain qe.devcluster.openshift.com --region us-east-2 --release-image quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.10.30-x86_64 -o yaml > onan.yaml

2.Edit inatsll template,and create cluster.

$ cat onan.yaml
apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  metadata:
    annotations:       
      hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image: "false"
    creationTimestamp: null
    labels:
      hive.openshift.io/hiveutil-created: "true"
 ....
      installerImageOverride: jshu.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:139926a3c3e93b89fa82c84b668029ee9619c8805e898267062ea5878b6a3c7d

$oc create -f onan.yaml
3.Install cluster ,check cd.Status.CLIImage domain  != cd.Status.InstallerImage domain.
steps.expectedResult:
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster1 -o json | jq -r ".status.installerImage"
jshu.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:139926a3c3e93b89fa82c84b668029ee9619c8805e898267062ea5878b6a3c7d
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster1 -o json | jq -r ".status.cliImage"
quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:9f92a648938f9a6a07c8af2f6a2532d94dacfc5076d609c30526a71deeee2a59

steps.step:
Scenario 3:
With the annotation hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image="true", installerImageOverride unset

1.Create cluster ,set annotation,unset the installerImageOverride.

$ ./bin/hiveutil create-cluster --cloud=aws jshucluster9 --base-domain qe.devcluster.openshift.com --region us-east-2 --release-image quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-release:4.10.30-x86_64 -o yaml > onan3.yaml

2.Edit inatsll template,and create cluster.
$ cat onan3.yaml
apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  kind: ClusterDeployment
  metadata:
    annotations:
      hive.openshift.io/cli-domain-from-installer-image: "true"
    creationTimestamp: null
    labels:
      hive.openshift.io/hiveutil-created: "true"
    name: jshucluster9
 
$oc create -f onan3.yaml
3.Install cluster ,check cd.Status.CLIImage domain and cd.Status.InstallerImage domain unchange.
steps.expectedResult:
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster9 -o json | jq -r ".status.installerImage"
quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:bd0e7a5e84e7a07fbf6b6602cc7d4486bb5907637ba33f138acbbc369e942681
[hmx@dhcp-140-15 hive]$ oc get cd jshucluster9 -o json | jq -r ".status.cliImage"
quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:9f92a648938f9a6a07c8af2f6a2532d94dacfc5076d609c30526a71deeee2a59

steps.step:
1.Install hive-operator
#way 1 (covered in OCP-29695)
  #Login to cluster web console with kubeadmin user, and go to OperatorHub menu, filter by "hive", click "OpenShift Hive"and enter.
 #Click the "install" button, then click "Subscribe",and wait a minute
#way 2
# ./oc create ns hive
namespace/hive created
# cat og.yaml 
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1
kind: OperatorGroup
metadata:
  name: hive-og
  namespace: hive
spec:
  targetNamespaces:
  - hive
# ./oc create -f og.yaml 
operatorgroup.operators.coreos.com/hive-og created
# cat sub.yaml 
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: Subscription
metadata:
  name: hive-sub
  namespace: hive
spec:
  channel: alpha
  name: hive-operator
  approval: Automatic 
  source: community-operators
  sourceNamespace: openshift-marketplace
# ./oc create -f sub.yaml 
subscription.operators.coreos.com/hive-sub created

2.Watch your operator come up,run:
$oc get csv -n openshift-operators
steps.expectedResult:
$ oc get csv -n openshift-operators
NAME                   DISPLAY          VERSION   REPLACES   PHASE
hive-operator.v1.0.1   OpenShift Hive   1.0.1                Succeeded

steps.step:
3.Verify the hive-operator is created in openshift-operators namespace,run
$oc get pods -n openshift-operators
steps.expectedResult:
$ oc get pods -n openshift-operators
NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
hive-operator-697756c9c8-wrg9x   1/1     Running   0          90m

steps.step:
4.Create a cluster-scoped HiveConfig CR to configure hive,  move to "Installed Operators->OpenShift Hive->Hive Config", and click "Create Hive Config" button, and put the below text to it and click "Create"
 
apiVersion: hive.openshift.io/v1
  kind: HiveConfig
  metadata:
    name: hive
  spec:
    logLevel: debug
  targetNamespace: hive

5.Verify Hive's pods are created in hive namespace,run,
$oc get pods -n hive
steps.expectedResult:
$ oc get pods -n hive
NAME                                  READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
hive-controllers-667bbf8b99-wqjpt     1/1     Running     0          72m
hiveadmission-5d65dcb7b9-f8s28        1/1     Running     0          72m
hiveadmission-5d65dcb7b9-r85sz        1/1     Running     0          72m

steps.step:
6. Verify if you are able to see the log message in the below format
$ oc logs -n hive hiveadmission-5d65dc7b9-r85sz | head -1
steps.expectedResult:
$ oc logs -n hive hiveadmission-5d65dc7b9-r85sz | head -1
time="2022-05-10T18:52:16Z" level=info msg="Version: openshift/hive v1.2.3633-595563c"

$./bin/hiveutil create-cluster lwan --base-domain qe.devcluster.openshift.com  --release-image registry.svc.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.5.0-0.nightly-2020-03-10-002435
